era_context: Late Bronze Age collapse period, advanced stage. The major state systems of the Eastern Mediterranean are in severe crisis or collapse. Egypt remains a major power but is economically strained. The Hittite Empire has effectively ceased to exist as a unified state. Mycenaean Greece is in the final stages of palatial collapse. The Eastern Mediterranean trade network is severely disrupted. This is a period of profound systemic transformation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (limited synchronisation with Old World chronology)

events.title: Ugarit destroyed and abandoned
events.description: The city of Ugarit, a major Bronze Age trade centre and diplomatic hub, is destroyed and abandoned. Archaeological evidence shows destruction layers with evidence of fire and violence. The destruction is dated to the Late Bronze Age collapse period, though the precise date and cause remain debated. Ugarit's destruction marks the end of a major centre of Bronze Age civilisation and disrupts Eastern Mediterranean trade networks. The city is not significantly reoccupied in the Iron Age.
events.certainty_note: Destruction is archaeologically confirmed, but the precise dating is debated. Conventional dating places destruction c. 1185 BCE, but some scholars argue for earlier dates. The cause (invasion, earthquake, internal conflict) is also debated.
cross_references: Part of broader Bronze Age collapse

events.title: Carchemish becomes a Neo-Hittite centre
events.description: Carchemish, a major city on the Euphrates, emerges as a centre of Neo-Hittite power following the collapse of the Hittite Empire. The city becomes the capital of a Neo-Hittite state that will dominate northern Syria and southern Anatolia in the Iron Age. This represents the transformation of Hittite political authority from a centralised empire to a network of regional city-states.
events.certainty_note: The emergence of Carchemish as a Neo-Hittite centre is archaeologically attested, but the precise dating of the transition is uncertain. The process likely occurred over several decades.

events.title: Mycenaean Greece enters the Dark Ages
events.description: The final Mycenaean palatial centres are abandoned or severely depopulated. The Linear B writing system ceases to be used. The complex bureaucratic and economic systems that sustained Mycenaean civilisation have collapsed. Greece enters a period of reduced population, simplified material culture, and loss of literacy that will last for several centuries (the Greek Dark Ages, c. 1100–800 BCE). This marks the end of the Bronze Age in Greece.
events.certainty_note: The collapse of Mycenaean civilisation is archaeologically confirmed. Dating is secure within ±10 years.

events.title: Climate stress and environmental disruption
events.description: Evidence from ice cores, tree rings, and sediment cores suggests significant climate stress during this period. A volcanic eruption (possibly Hekla in Iceland or another source) may have caused a period of cooler, drier conditions in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. This environmental stress is hypothesised to have contributed to crop failures, famine, and population displacement, exacerbating the political and military crises of the Bronze Age collapse. However, the relative importance of climate stress versus other factors remains debated.

sources.name: Ice core data (GISP2, Greenland Ice Sheet Project)

events.certainty_note: Paleoclimatic evidence suggests climate stress during this period, but the precise dating and magnitude of the stress are debated. The causal relationship between climate stress and civilisational collapse is hypothetical.
cross_references: Related to all Bronze Age collapse events

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Ugarit's destruction is contested. Some scholars place it c. 1185 BCE (conventional chronology), while others argue for earlier dates. The cause of destruction is also debated: some scholars emphasise invasion by Sea Peoples, others emphasise earthquake or internal conflict. The role of climate stress in the Bronze Age collapse is increasingly recognised by scholars, but some argue that climate stress was a secondary factor rather than a primary cause. Additionally, the 'Bronze Age collapse' is itself a historiographic construct; the process was gradual and uneven across different regions, not a sudden, synchronous collapse.
historiographic_note: Sources for this year are primarily archaeological, supplemented by fragmentary written records (Egyptian temple inscriptions, Ugaritic texts, Hittite inscriptions). There is no contemporary narrative account of the collapse itself. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East; other regions are poorly documented. The causes of the Bronze Age collapse are inferred from material evidence and paleoclimatic data rather than contemporary accounts. There is significant scholarly debate about the relative importance of military invasion, climate stress, systems collapse, and other factors. The perspective is inevitably weighted toward the regions with the richest archaeological records (Egypt, Levant, Anatolia, Greece).

graph_edges.note: Ugarit's destruction is part of the same Sea Peoples crisis
Carchemish's emergence as a Neo-Hittite centre follows the Hittite Empire's collapse
Final collapse of Mycenaean civilisation follows the earlier decline
Climate stress is hypothesised to have contributed to Ugarit's destruction and other Bronze Age collapse events
